Hornfel Review - Empires and Puzzles Construct Family Hero Strategy Guide

Hornfel brings a unique 'Knockdown' mechanic to the arena. Does stopping tile damage make him a defensive juggernaut, or is he just a stone bull in a china shop? We break down the math behind his Construct Core and that confusing skill description.
Hornfel, The Magma Minotaur: Smashing Tiles & Delaying Doom

Hornfel is a 5-star Fire hero from the Construct family, originally released in early 2024. He enters the arena as a hulking stone minotaur, bringing a completely unique mechanic to the game: "Knockdown." While many heroes simply kill you, Hornfel tries to stop you from playing the game—specifically by messing with your ability to deal tile damage or normal attacks. He is a Barbarian, which means his bleed damage can add up, but let's be honest, we're here for the magma and the mechanics.

Hornfel is a visual treat—a massive, humanoid bull hewn from stone, with veins of molten magma pulsing through cracks in his armor. He looks like he walked straight out of a volcano's nightmare. The "Construct" aesthetic is strong here; he doesn't look born, he looks
. It's a great design that communicates "tanky bruiser" before you even look at the stats. Just don't ask him to sneak around; he's about as subtle as a meteor strike.
Released in 2024, Hornfel's base stats (1021 Attack / 1110 Defense / 1872 Health) were impressive for their time but now sit firmly in the "solid but aging" category for the 2026 meta.
1021 is respectable, but modern attackers often push 1300+. He relies heavily on his buffs and Core damage to compete.

With over 1100 Defense and nearly 1900 Health, he is naturally bulky. However, compared to the latest behemoths boasting 2200+ Health base, he needs Limit Breaks (LB1 and LB2) to survive the initial onslaught of modern fast-speed snipers.
"Bulwark Charge" is a Fast speed skill that does three things: it hits, it buffs, and it confuses people. Let's clarify the
effect, which changes based on where Hornfel is standing:
If Hornfel is on your Defense team and hits an enemy, that enemy (and their neighbors)
do not contribute to Troop damage
If he hits your 3 blue heroes, matching blue tiles will deal almost zero damage (effectively base 1 damage) for 2 turns. This is a
counter to mono-color stacks. It forces attackers to rely solely on Special Skills to kill him during that window.
If you are using Hornfel and he hits an enemy, their

The enemy won't do their turn-based auto-attack. It's a nice little survival tool, especially against high-attack titans or hard-hitting bosses, but in PvP raids, it's often negligible compared to stopping their mana generation.
+50% Attack to himself and nearby allies for 3 turns.
This is huge. A 50% attack buff at Fast speed turns him and his neighbors into wrecking balls, significantly boosting tile damage and subsequent special skills.
Fast. He needs 8 tiles to charge. With a level 29 Magic/Styx troop or sufficient mana nodes + a bard/costume bonus, he can easily drop to 7 tiles (6 with extreme setups/Rush), making him very spammy.
When fully charged (typically after taking 5 hits or casting 5 times), this adds a ~180% AoE hit to
If the Core fires with the Special, you are looking at 480% (Skill) + 180% (Core) =
and substantial damage to everyone else. That is sniper-level damage on a "Hit-3" hero.

Negating tile damage for 2 turns is statistically powerful. In a mono-stack scenario, tile damage accounts for ~70% of the damage output. Removing that for 2 turns can buy your team critical survival time.
Family Bonus, Passives, Aether Power & Unique Abilities
The Construct family is defined by their "Core" mechanic, which essentially gives them a second, passive Special Skill.
The Construct Core charges when the hero receives damage or uses a special skill. This rewards Hornfel for being in the line of fire (Tank/Flank position).
This is Hornfel's secret weapon. It deals damage to
enemies when it discharges. It scales with charge, but even a partial charge adds free damage. It effectively makes him an AoE hitter disguised as a Hit-3 hero.
He is immune to Silence or Mindless Attack effects that block specials (like Miki, Peters, or Alfrike's mindless attack
it's considered a block, though usually it's mana control). Note: He is
immune to Mana Stop (Hel/Proteus) unless specifically stated, but "Blocking" usually refers to the Silence debuff.

At the start of battle, he gets a 15% attack boost, increasing every time he is hit (up to +25%). This synergizes perfectly with his +50% Attack buff skill. If he fires while Enraged, his damage output spikes dramatically.

Upon release, Hornfel was a "wait, what does he do?" hero. The Knockdown mechanic was misunderstood. Players eventually realized his defensive utility against tile damage was strong, but his offensive utility (stopping slash attacks) was weak.

In the modern era of 2026, Hornfel's stats are average. He isn't the impenetrable wall he might have been, but his damage output remains surprisingly high thanks to the Core + Attack Buff combination.
He struggles to survive against modern 1-shot snipers.
He is still a beast. In
tournaments, his fast speed and buffs make him excellent.
He sees play here! Stopping the Titan's slash attack (which can one-shot squishy heroes) every few turns is actually a very valid survival strategy for pushing high-level titans.
Hornfel is a Barbarian. You generally want to keep him alive to fire his buff and charge his core, but his damage scaling is also excellent.
He acts as a meat shield. The longer he lives, the more his Core charges from taking hits, and the more likely he is to fire his tile-nerfing skill.
Maximizes the 480% hit and the 180% Core damage. With his self-buff and Rage, a high-attack Hornfel can devastate a team.
He might die before firing in the current high-damage meta.
. His primary unique value is utility (stopping tiles/slash attacks) and passive Core damage. He needs to survive to be annoying.
Unique ability to render enemy tiles useless for 2 turns (on Defense).
The Construct Core adds significant AoE damage that doesn't appear on the card's main text.
+50% Attack is a top-tier buff value for a Fast hero.
One of the few heroes who can mitigate Titan slash attacks consistently.
The "Knockdown" effect on offense (delaying slash attacks) is often irrelevant in fast-paced raids.
He takes heavy damage from modern snipers.
To maximize his defensive utility, he needs to hit the right enemies (usually the ones with a stack of tiles under them).
Very Fast Hornfel is a nightmare. He fires constantly, keeping the enemy team permanently "Knocked Down" and unable to use tiles effectively.
The slash attack delay saves items (Time Stops) and heroes.
As a Tank or Flank, he punishes mono-attackers who rely on tiles.
Hornfel is a team player. He wants to be around heroes who benefit from his Attack buff and who can help him survive.

Place him at Tank. If he fires, the attacker can't use tiles to kill him. Flank him with fast snipers (like Marjana-C or newer red hitters) who benefit from the +50% Attack buff to wipe the enemy team.
Hornfel keeps the team alive by stopping slash attacks, while the others stack defense down.
Malosi or Mist can stop his "Knockdown" and Buffs.
If you are attacking Hornfel, bring a cleanser (Rigard, etc.) to remove the "Knockdown" status so your tiles do damage again.
Hornfel doesn't deal well with "meat shields" blocking his hits.

If you pulled Hornfel, congratulations! He is a fantastic aggressive tank for early-to-mid game. Max him out. Focus on using him in Raids to disable key threats or on Defense to annoy your opponents. Don't worry too much about the complex wording; just know that when he hits people, they struggle to hit back.

Hornfel is a luxury hero in 2026. He's not a "must-have" for top 100 play, but he is a fun "gotcha" card for Alliance Wars. If you have Alpha Aethers to spare, his Rage power makes him a surprisingly hard-hitting flank. However, if you have the latest 2025/26 powerhouse reds, he likely sits on your bench for War Cleanups or Rush Tournaments.
When Hornfel launched, the forums were... confused.
“I’m still very confused about the knock-down effect... Is removing contribution to troop damage basically like reducing their damage for 2 turns?”
— Many players were disappointed that he didn't stop Special Skills, only normal attacks.
“He’s a very strong hero. Very cool mechanic... damage is insane.”
— Supporters pointed out that despite the weird utility, his raw damage numbers (Core + Skill) were top tier at the time.

Hornfel is the definition of a "Bruiser." He isn't the most elegant hero, and his text box requires a law degree to decipher, but he gets the job done. He smashes faces, buffs his friends, and tells the enemy tiles to sit down and be quiet. He might be getting older, but a charging stone minotaur is never something you can ignore completely.
A solid B+ hero in the modern era, elevated to A- in Rush Tournaments.
